title: CS50 2D - Lecture 7 - Pokémon
summary: CS50 2D is Harvard's course on game development using Love2D, with the final lecture covering Pokémon-inspired RPG mechanics including state stacks, GUI design, and turn-based battle systems. The course is available on edX, Harvard Extension School, and Harvard Summer School.
writeup: CS50G Lecture 7 (the final lecture) uses a Pokemon clone built in Lua and Love2D to introduce state stacks, GUI composition, and turn-based RPG mechanics. The central argument is that a flat state machine is insufficient for layered game states such as a battle screen that preserves an underlying field state, and that a push/pop stack solves this cleanly. The lecture proceeds from a live student demo through tile-based world design, random encounter triggering, and the construction of dialogue boxes, menus, and health/XP progress bars as composable GUI elements.
key_points: The state stack replaces a flat state machine for games with layered states: pushing a battle state onto the field state preserves the field exactly, and popping the battle state returns the player to their exact prior position without any variable-passing gymnastics.

Random wild encounters are tied to tile type: the game renders two grass layers (flat and tall), and every step taken while the player is in the walk state on a tall-grass tile rolls a chance to push a battle state.

GUI elements in this implementation are all composable building blocks: dialogue boxes render a rectangle with text, menus expose arrow-key-navigable options, and health and XP progress bars are tween-interpolated elements that share the same timer infrastructure already present in Love2D.

The turn-based battle system alternates player and enemy turns, resolves damage from stat comparisons, grants XP on victory (with level-up over repeated fights), and branches into a distinct faint/revive state when the player's Pokemon is knocked out.

The lecture explicitly acknowledges that this implementation covers only the field and battle states as a core deliverable, with larger menu states left as problem set work, illustrating how to scope a complex system by identifying its essential state transitions first.

chapters.title: GUI Elements: Dialogue Boxes and Menus
Tile Layers and Random Encounter Logic
